title: If your first backtest is green, I don’t trust it
author: MarketStructureGuy

comments.author: Content-Ad-1246
comments.body: When can you really trust your backtest?

replies.author: MarketStructureGuy
replies.body:
You don’t trust a backtest because it’s green.
You start trusting it when it survives being attacked.
A backtest earns credibility when:
It stays profitable after parameter degradation (worse fills, wider stops, higher fees)
It holds up across multiple regimes (trend, chop, volatility expansion)
It’s tested out-of-sample and forward-walked
It still works when you remove the best 10–20% of trades
Drawdowns make sense structurally, not just statistically
If your first backtest is clean and smooth, it’s usually curve-fit.
Real systems look ugly before they look trustworthy.
Green isn’t the signal.
Fragility is.
